_schema._note: Analytical framework for evaluating Berkeley's General Fund (~$292M) spending against a core/non-core taxonomy. Used to classify agenda items and assess whether council members' legislative output moves toward or away from structural fiscal repair. Dollar estimates derived from FY2025-2026 operating budget. Not a line-item audit — a structural map.
_schema._reference_standard: The structural deficit is documented in consecutive City Manager budget messages using the phrase 'not sustainable' and in City Auditor findings naming a $32-33M structural gap. The framework below identifies where the gap could be closed through delivery model changes and non-core program rollbacks — independently of political feasibility.
_schema.carry_cost_principle: Every in-house employee generates obligations that outlast the service: CalPERS pension accrual, OPEB (retiree health), and a share of the unfunded actuarial accrued liability (UAAL). The true cost of an in-house position is salary + current pension contribution + UAAL share + OPEB — not just salary. When a service is contracted out or transferred, new liability accrual stops, but the existing pension tail for current and former employees persists for decades. This means: (1) the full savings from delivery model changes materialize slowly; (2) each year of inaction adds more employees whose obligations compound; (3) the cost of delay is not linear — it grows.

classification_values.adds_non_core: Agenda item increases spending in a non-core category — moves away from structural repair
classification_values.reduces_non_core: Agenda item reduces or restructures non-core spending — moves toward structural repair
classification_values.addresses_cost_premium: Agenda item changes delivery model of a core function to reduce cost premium
classification_values.entrenches_cost_premium: Agenda item locks in expensive in-house delivery model (new hires, expanded contracts, new facilities for in-house services)
classification_values.p1_direct: Agenda item directly addresses a documented P1 problem (infrastructure backlog, structural deficit, CalPERS/OPEB, reserve policy)
classification_values.neutral: Core operations, routine maintenance, regulatory compliance — neither adds non-core nor addresses structural problem
classification_values.revenue_seeking: Proposes new tax or bond without accompanying spending reduction — treats revenue as substitute for structural reform

categories.label: Public health — city-run layer
categories.description: City-run clinics, outreach, and health programming that duplicates Alameda County Public Health Department's statutory mandate.
categories.alternative: Transfer staff (not management layers) to county employment; negotiate graduated step-down of city contribution to peer-city levels over 3-5 years. Not a cliff — a ramp.
categories.carry_cost_note: City health employees carry full CalPERS + OPEB obligations. Transfer to county stops new city liability accrual but does not eliminate the tail for existing employees. Savings materialize gradually as transferred positions roll off city payroll.
categories.political_note: Requires county agreement and intergovernmental negotiation. Staff transfer (not layoff) reduces union opposition. Management reduction is where resistance concentrates.
categories.constraint: Service level depends on county absorbing capacity. County is not currently resourced to absorb without transition funding.

categories.label: Housing / homeless services — direct service model
categories.description: City-funded outreach, case management, and direct services. Represents one layer of a three-layer problem: (1) direct service delivery cost, (2) nonprofit contract management overhead, (3) outcomes accountability gap. These are separate problems with different reform mechanics.

sub_components.direct_city_staff: City employees providing services — CalPERS + OPEB tail applies
sub_components.nonprofit_pass_through: Contracts to nonprofits — can be reduced or restructured without pension carry cost, but contract terms and political relationships create friction
sub_components.contract_management_overhead: City staff managing contracts — often grows proportionally with contract volume without proportional outcome improvement

categories.alternative: Shift toward vouchers and county-led programs; restructure nonprofit contracts around outcomes rather than inputs; reduce direct city staffing.
categories.carry_cost_note: City employees in HHCS carry full pension tail. Nonprofit contract reductions have no pension carry cost — savings are immediate. This distinction matters for sequencing: nonprofit contract reform is faster and cleaner than staff reductions.
categories.political_note: Highest political resistance of any category. Nonprofit ecosystem has organized advocacy infrastructure and council relationships. Outcomes data is contested.
categories.constraint: Visible service reduction. Demand does not decrease with funding. County and state systems are not substitutes in the short run.

categories.label: Workforce development / youth programming
categories.description: Job training, youth worker programs, summer employment.
categories.alternative: Community colleges, nonprofits, state workforce boards (EDD, regional boards).
categories.carry_cost_note: If city-staffed, carries pension tail. If nonprofit-contracted, no carry cost — reductions are immediate.
categories.constraint: Gap between what state/county programs offer and what Berkeley currently funds. Some programs have no direct substitute at current quality level.

categories.label: Food programs / direct assistance
categories.description: Summer lunch programs, food security grants.
categories.alternative: School districts (BUSD), nonprofits, federal programs (SNAP, WIC, CACFP).
categories.carry_cost_note: Primarily grant pass-through — minimal pension carry cost. Reductions are relatively clean.
categories.constraint: Federal program access requires eligibility compliance. Gap coverage depends on nonprofit capacity.

categories.label: Cultural / nonprofit grant ecosystem
categories.carry_cost_note: Pure grant pass-through — no city pension carry cost. Reductions are immediate and clean.
categories.constraint: Arts organizations dependent on city grants lack alternative revenue infrastructure in the short run. Longer runway is more feasible.

categories.label: Recreation subsidies — non-maintenance
categories.description: Camps, scholarships, program-heavy recreation subsidies (not facility maintenance).
categories.alternative: Fee-for-service model with means-tested subsidy for low-income access.
categories.carry_cost_note: City recreation staff carry CalPERS + OPEB. Shifting to fee-for-service doesn't eliminate existing staff obligations — it reduces future hiring and may enable attrition. A hybrid model (partial fee recovery) is more realistic than full cost recovery.
categories.constraint: Equity concern: fee-for-service without subsidy program excludes low-income families. Means-tested subsidy preserves access at lower city cost.

categories.label: Alternative public safety layers
categories.description: Violence prevention programs, wellness response layers, non-police crisis response.
categories.alternative: No direct substitute. Either absorbed into police response or eliminated.
categories.carry_cost_note: If city-staffed, carries pension tail. Program staff added in recent years represent obligations that persist even if programs are restructured.
categories.constraint: Political framing as public safety alternative makes cuts visible and contested. Some programs have evidence base; others do not — outcomes data is the lever.

categories.label: Transportation behavior programs
categories.description: Vision Zero overlays, bike/pedestrian initiatives beyond baseline infrastructure maintenance.
categories.alternative: No substitute. Pure policy overlay — removing it means the activity stops.
categories.carry_cost_note: Staff in this area carry pension tail. Program reductions don't eliminate existing obligations.
categories.constraint: Strong advocacy constituency. Some components (basic signal timing, crosswalk maintenance) are arguably core — the non-core portion is the program overlay, not the infrastructure maintenance.

categories.label: Climate / sustainability administration
categories.description: Electrification programs, climate staffing, sustainability overlays not required for baseline operations.
categories.carry_cost_note: Climate staff added in recent years carry pension tail. Positions added during the fiscal crisis period represent obligations that predate any structural repair.
categories.constraint: Politically high-salience. State mandates create some non-discretionary compliance floor, but the discretionary overlay above that floor is material.

categories.label: Expanded governance layer
categories.description: Legislative aides, commission support staff, community engagement processes.
categories.carry_cost_note: Every staff position in this layer carries pension tail. Governance expansion during the fiscal crisis compounds the structural problem directly — these are obligations that will outlast the programs they support.
categories.constraint: Council members are direct consumers of this spending — restructuring requires self-imposed restraint.

categories.label: Equity / targeted program layers
categories.description: Targeted scholarships, parallel access programs, equity-focused service overlays.
categories.carry_cost_note: Mix of staff and contract. Staff components carry pension tail; contract components can be reduced more cleanly.
categories.constraint: High political visibility. Framing as equity programs makes reduction politically costly regardless of evidence base.

categories.label: Solid waste — municipalized vs. contracted
categories.description: Berkeley employs its own trash collectors. No comparable city near Berkeley runs municipal solid waste collection in-house. The premium reflects compensation + pension structure relative to franchised hauler model.
categories.alternative: Contracted private haulers (standard model in comparable California cities).
categories.carry_cost_note: This is the starkest carry cost case. Each municipal solid waste employee represents decades of CalPERS + OPEB obligation. Contracting out stops new accrual, but the pension tail for existing workers persists. The full savings from outsourcing would not materialize for 15-20 years as current employees retire. However, stopping the accrual of new obligations is still structurally valuable — delay makes the problem larger, not smaller.
categories.political_note: High strike risk. SEIU or Teamsters representation means any outsourcing attempt triggers protected labor action. Realistic path is attrition-based: contract out incremental new work rather than existing positions, allow headcount to decline naturally.
categories.constraint: Transition requires franchise agreement, RFP process, service continuity planning. 2-3 year lead time minimum.

categories.label: Public works — in-house vs. contract mix
categories.description: Street maintenance, tree work, fleet — partially in-house where contract delivery would be cheaper.
categories.alternative: Outsourced project-based delivery for non-routine work.
categories.carry_cost_note: In-house public works staff carry full pension tail. Each position added during the fiscal crisis represents obligations that persist for decades.
categories.constraint: Some in-house capacity is genuinely cost-effective for routine/recurring work. The premium is in project and specialized work that could be contracted.

categories.label: Fire staffing model
categories.description: Overtime-heavy staffing structure. Fire is a safety member with the highest CalPERS contribution rates (~50%+ of salary).
categories.alternative: Staffing model changes to reduce chronic overtime; regionalization is theoretically possible but rare.
categories.carry_cost_note: Safety member pension obligations are the most severe carry cost in the city. Each firefighter represents a pension obligation that extends 30-40 years post-retirement. The overtime model compounds this: overtime pay is pensionable, meaning current overtime choices directly increase future unfunded liability.
categories.constraint: BFFA contract limits operational flexibility. Staffing minimums are contractually bound.

categories.label: Police cost structure
categories.description: Compensation, staffing ratios, specialized units relative to peer city benchmarks.
categories.alternative: Limited external options. Primarily internal reform: civilianization of non-sworn roles, unit restructuring.
categories.carry_cost_note: Safety member pension at highest CalPERS tier. Same overtime-pensionable problem as fire. Any new sworn officer hired today represents a 30-40 year pension obligation at safety rates.
categories.constraint: BPA contract limits restructuring. Political environment makes police reduction proposals high-visibility.

categories.label: Administrative overhead
categories.description: Larger organizational footprint than peer cities, driven by program layer complexity. Management ratios above comparable jurisdictions.
categories.alternative: Span-of-control review; headcount reduction through attrition as programs consolidate.
categories.carry_cost_note: Management staff typically at top of pension accrual. Reducing management headcount is both the highest-value and slowest-to-realize reform — pensions vest and obligations persist regardless of employment status.
categories.constraint: Depends on program consolidation in categories 1 and 2 — overhead shrinks as program layers shrink, not before.

summary.key_insight: The structural deficit ($32-33M) is fully addressable within the non-core substitutable category alone, without touching core functions or politically explosive delivery model changes. The gap is a policy choice, not a resource constraint.

sequencing.note_fastest: Pure grant pass-through with no city pension tail. Reductions are immediate. Political resistance is moderate.
sequencing.note_medium: Staff components require attrition or transfer. 2-4 year horizon. 1A requires intergovernmental negotiation.
sequencing.note_long_term: Delivery model changes and the largest non-core bucket. 5-10 year horizon. Pension tail means savings materialize slowly even when changes are made.
sequencing.political_sequencing_note: Program consolidation in categories 1 and 2 is a prerequisite for administrative overhead reduction in 3E — the overhead exists because the programs exist. Sequence matters: don't expect administrative savings before program decisions.
